video_title: Conservation of linear momentum | AP Physics | Khan Academy

segments.text: If an object has a mass m and it's moving velocity v,
then it has a momentum, which is given as the product of the mass times velocity.
It's a vector quantity, it has the same direction as that of the velocity.
And why do we care about momentum?
Because according to Newton's second law, the net force acting on an object
equals the rate at which its momentum is changing.
So here's a question.
What will happen if the net force on an object is zero?
Imagine that this box is moving on a perfectly frictionless surface,
and there is no air or anything like that.
Well, if the net force is zero for some time, then over that time period,
the change in momentum must also be zero.
In other words, the momentum of this crate should be a constant.
In other words, the crate would continue to move with that constant velocity
as long as the net force on it is zero.
This is not new.
If net force on an object is zero, its velocity stays a constant.
We already know that.
But here's where it gets interesting.
This idea doesn't just apply to one particle.
It can apply to a system of particles as well.
Here's what I mean.
Imagine instead of one crate, we have two crates of some masses
moving towards each other with some velocities.
Let's consider the system of these two particles.
How can we extend this idea to a system?
Well, now, if the net external force on my system is zero,
then it turns out that the momentum of this system,
which basically is the sum of individual momentum.
If there are two particles, it's just p1 plus p2,
but if there are more, then you can just add them all up vectorially.
The momentum of the system would be a constant.
This is called the conservation of linear momentum,
and it's one of the cornerstones in physics.
But what is it really trying to tell us?
Think about it.
If these two crates were to come and hit each other,
and let's say, after hitting each other, they will stick to each other.
Now, during this collision, they are putting forces on each other.
But these are internal forces.
The particles are interacting amongst themselves.
There is no net external force on any of them.
So the conservation of linear momentum says,
even though they are colliding with each other,
and their individual speeds are changing,
and their individual moment are changing,
the momentum of the system stays the same even after the collision,
because there is no net external force acting on it.
That's what this principle says.
Take any system, and it doesn't matter how they are interacting with each other,
it doesn't matter how complicated those interactions are.
As long as there is no external force acting on that system,
the system's momentum cannot change.
It has to stay a constant.
And by the way, we call it the conservation of linear momentum,
because there is another kind of momentum called angular momentum.
There is a conservation principle associated with that as well,
but that's not something we're talking about.
Whenever we say momentum, we're talking about linear momentum.

Let's see if we can use this to predict what their velocity is going to be
once they stick to each other.

the momentum stays the same.

let's just consider one direction to be positive.
